events.title: Founding of Tenochtitlan
events.detail: According to Mexica tradition, the god Huitzilopochtli guided the wandering Aztec people to an island in Lake Texcoco where they spotted an eagle perched on a cactus devouring a serpent — the divine sign marking where they should build their city. On March 13, 1325 (traditional date), they founded Tenochtitlan. The island city was connected to the mainland by three major causeways and grew to house an estimated 200,000–300,000 people — one of the largest cities in the world at the time.

events.title: Acamapichtli: First Tlatoani of Tenochtitlan
events.detail: Acamapichtli became the first tlatoani (ruler) of Tenochtitlan in 1372, establishing the dynastic lineage that would rule the city until the Spanish Conquest. Under his rule, Tenochtitlan began consolidating power and expanding its agricultural base through the construction of chinampas (floating gardens) in the lake.

events.title: Triple Alliance Formed — Aztec Empire Begins
events.detail: Under Itzcoatl, Tenochtitlan formed the Triple Alliance with Texcoco and Tlacopan, defeating the dominant Tepanec empire of Azcapotzalco. This alliance became the foundation of the Aztec Empire (Mexica Empire), with Tenochtitlan as its dominant partner. The alliance collected tribute from dozens of city-states and ultimately controlled territory from the Gulf of Mexico to the Pacific.

events.title: Great Temple (Templo Mayor) Reconsecration
events.detail: Under Ahuitzotl, the expanded Templo Mayor was reconsecrated in an unprecedented ceremony. Spanish chronicler Juan de Torquemada later claimed 80,400 captives were sacrificed over four days — a figure modern scholars debate, but the event represented the height of Aztec religious power and the city's status as the empire's sacred and political center. The Templo Mayor's ruins were rediscovered beneath Mexico City's historic center in 1978.

sources.name: Fray Bernardino de Sahagún — Historia general de las cosas de la Nueva España

events.title: Moctezuma II Ascends to Power
events.detail: Moctezuma Xocoyotzin became the ninth and final tlatoani of the independent Aztec Empire in 1502. He expanded the empire to its greatest extent and centralized power in Tenochtitlan. By the time of Spanish contact (1519), the city had a population of 200,000–300,000 and elaborate water, sewage, and market infrastructure that astonished Spanish observers.

sources.name: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — Historia verdadera de la conquista

events.title: Hernán Cortés Enters Tenochtitlan
events.detail: On November 8, 1519, Hernán Cortés and 400 Spanish soldiers, allied with Tlaxcalan warriors, peacefully entered Tenochtitlan, welcomed by Moctezuma II. Cortés described the city in letters to King Charles V as larger and better organized than European capitals. Shortly after, Cortés placed Moctezuma under house arrest, sparking a crisis. Moctezuma died in June 1520 under contested circumstances — Spaniards claimed the crowd killed him; indigenous sources say Spaniards did.

sources.name: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — Historia verdadera

events.title: La Noche Triste — Spaniards Flee Tenochtitlan
events.detail: On June 30, 1520, Mexica warriors drove Cortés and his forces out of Tenochtitlan during a chaotic night retreat across the causeway to Tlacopan. An estimated 600–800 Spanish soldiers and thousands of Tlaxcalan allies drowned or were killed in the escape. Cortés reportedly wept beneath a large tree in what is now the Popotla neighborhood of Tacuba. The Spanish consolidated their forces in Tlaxcala and returned with a siege.

sources.name: Bernal Díaz del Castillo — Historia verdadera

events.title: Fall of Tenochtitlan
events.detail: After a 75-day siege combining Spanish forces and Tlaxcalan, Texcocan, and other indigenous allies (totaling ~200,000), Tenochtitlan fell on August 13, 1521. The last Aztec ruler, Cuauhtémoc, was captured while attempting to flee by canoe. The city was largely destroyed in the fighting. Estimates of deaths from combat, smallpox epidemic, famine, and siege range from 100,000 to 240,000. The Spanish began building Mexico City directly atop Tenochtitlan's ruins.

events.title: Mexico City Founded on Tenochtitlan Ruins
events.detail: Following the conquest, Hernán Cortés ordered Mexico City built directly atop the rubble of Tenochtitlan, reusing its stone and labor. The colonial grid plan was imposed over the Mexica street layout, and indigenous workers were forced to construct the cathedral, the cabildo, and Cortés's own palace on the sacred precincts. The city became capital of the Viceroyalty of New Spain, the richest colonial possession in the Americas.

sources.name: INAH / Museo de la Ciudad de México

events.title: Apparition of Virgin of Guadalupe to Juan Diego
events.detail: In December 1531, indigenous Nahua convert Juan Diego reported four apparitions of the Virgin Mary at Tepeyac Hill in what is now northern Mexico City. The image miraculously imprinted on his tilma (cloak) became one of the most venerated icons in the Americas. The Basílica de Guadalupe built at the site became the most-visited Catholic pilgrimage destination in the world, drawing 20+ million visitors annually.

events.title: Royal University of Mexico Founded (UNAM Ancestor)
events.detail: King Charles I of Spain decreed the founding of the Real Universidad de México on September 21, 1551, making it one of the oldest universities in the Americas (predating Harvard by 85 years). It became the intellectual center of New Spain. In 1910, during the centennial of Independence, President Porfirio Díaz formally re-established it as the Universidad Nacional de México, later renamed UNAM — today Mexico's largest and most prestigious university with 350,000+ students.

events.title: Great Flood Inundates Mexico City for Five Years
events.detail: On September 21, 1629, heavy rains caused the lakes surrounding Mexico City to overflow, inundating the capital up to 2 meters deep. The flood lasted nearly five years (until 1634), killing an estimated 30,000 people and forcing the indigenous population to largely abandon the city. Spanish colonists debated relocating the capital entirely. The disaster accelerated drainage projects (desagüe) that would eventually drain the lake system entirely over subsequent centuries.

events.title: Corn Riot — First Major Urban Uprising
events.detail: On June 8, 1692, a combination of harvest failures, food shortages, and colonial exploitation triggered a massive riot in which a multiracial mob — indigenous workers, mestizos, and Afro-Mexicans — burned the viceregal palace, the city hall, and market stalls in the Zócalo. The uprising exposed deep social tensions in colonial Mexico City and led to reprisals but also some administrative reforms. Historian Carlos de Sigüenza y Góngora documented the event in detail.

sources.name: Carlos de Sigüenza y Góngora — Alboroto y Motín de los Indios de México
AGN / UNAM Instituto de Investigaciones Históricas

events.title: Grito de Independencia — War for Independence Begins
events.detail: In the early morning of September 16, 1810, Father Miguel Hidalgo rang the bell of his church in Dolores, Guanajuato, and called the people to arms against Spanish colonial rule — the Grito de Independencia. The uprising quickly spread toward Mexico City. Though Hidalgo's forces were defeated before reaching the capital, the 11-year independence war was underway. Today, September 15 is commemorated with the Grito ceremony at the Zócalo, where the Head of Government rings a bell from the Palacio Nacional balcony.

sources.name: Instituto Nacional de Estudios Históricos de las Revoluciones de México (INEHRM)

events.title: Mexican Independence — Mexico City Becomes Capital
events.detail: On September 27, 1821, Agustín de Iturbide led the Ejército Trigarante into Mexico City to cheering crowds, completing the independence from Spain. Mexico City was declared the capital of the new Mexican Empire and, after Iturbide's abdication in 1823, of the Federal Republic. The Zócalo and its surroundings were transformed from viceregal symbols to republican ones. The city had approximately 160,000 inhabitants.

sources.name: Museo Nacional de Historia — Castillo de Chapultepec

events.title: Battle of Chapultepec — Niños Héroes
events.detail: On September 13, 1847, during the Mexican-American War, U.S. forces stormed the Chapultepec Military Academy on the hill overlooking Mexico City. According to Mexican national mythology, six young cadets (Los Niños Héroes) chose to die rather than surrender, with the youngest — Juan Escutia — wrapping himself in the Mexican flag and jumping from the castle. The battle led to the U.S. occupation of Mexico City and the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo (1848), by which Mexico ceded half its territory.

sources.name: Museo Nacional de Historia — Castillo de Chapultepec

events.title: Maximilian's Empire — French Intervention in Mexico City
events.detail: In 1864, Habsburg Archduke Maximilian I of Austria arrived in Mexico City as Emperor, backed by French troops under Napoleon III and conservative Mexican elites. He transformed Chapultepec Castle into an imperial palace and commissioned the Paseo del Emperador (today's Paseo de la Reforma) as a grand boulevard. Despite modernizing reforms, Maximilian was opposed by liberal forces under Benito Juárez. He was captured, tried, and executed by firing squad in Querétaro in 1867. Juárez returned to Mexico City victorious.

sources.name: Museo Nacional de Historia — Castillo de Chapultepec

events.title: Porfiriato — Rapid Modernization of Mexico City
events.detail: Porfirio Díaz seized power in 1876 and ruled Mexico for 34 years (with one interruption). Under the Porfiriato, Mexico City was transformed into a modern capital: Paseo de la Reforma was expanded and lined with grand mansions, electric streetcars were introduced, the Gran Canal de Desagüe was completed (1900) to drain the lakes, and the Palacio de Bellas Artes and Monument to Independence were begun. The population grew from 200,000 to 470,000. But modernization came at the cost of indigenous land dispossession and extreme inequality.

sources.name: México a través de los siglos / UNAM

events.title: Ángel de la Independencia Inaugurated
events.detail: On September 16, 1910, during the centennial of Mexican Independence, Porfirio Díaz inaugurated the Columna de la Independencia (Ángel de la Independencia) on Paseo de la Reforma. Designed by architect Antonio Rivas Mercado, the 45-meter golden-winged column became Mexico City's most iconic monument. It was damaged by the 1957 and 1985 earthquakes and rebuilt. Today it serves as the city's de facto public gathering point for sports celebrations, protests, and national commemorations.

events.title: Mexican Revolution — Mexico City as Contested Capital
events.detail: Francisco I. Madero's Plan de San Luis Potosí launched the Mexican Revolution on November 20, 1910. Mexico City became the prize and battleground of the conflict. Díaz fled in May 1911. Madero entered the capital to popular acclaim. After Madero's assassination in the 'Decena Trágica' (February 1913), the city passed through Huerta's military dictatorship, then was occupied by Zapatista and Villista forces in 1914.

sources.name: INEHRM — Historia de la Revolución Mexicana

events.title: Zapata and Villa Occupy Mexico City
events.detail: In December 1914, Emiliano Zapata and Pancho Villa jointly occupied Mexico City following the collapse of Huerta's government. The two revolutionary leaders — representing the agrarian south and the north — met at the Zócalo in an iconic photograph. However, neither was interested in governing the capital, and they withdrew shortly after. The famous photo of Villa sitting in the presidential chair became one of the most reproduced images of the Revolution.

events.title: Constitution of 1917 — Most Progressive in the World
events.detail: On February 5, 1917, Mexico promulgated a new constitution at Querétaro that became one of the world's most progressive documents: guaranteeing land reform (agrarian rights), workers' rights (8-hour workday, unions), public education, and restrictions on the Church and foreign companies. For Mexico City, the constitution established federal governance of the Distrito Federal, with no elected local government — a structure that would persist until 1997. The constitution remains in force today.

sources.name: Constitución Política de los Estados Unidos Mexicanos (CPEUM)

events.title: PNR Founded — Beginning of 71-Year One-Party Rule
events.detail: In March 1929, President Plutarco Elías Calles founded the Partido Nacional Revolucionario (PNR) in Mexico City, unifying Mexico's warring revolutionary factions under one umbrella. The party evolved into the PRM (1938) and then the PRI (1946) — ruling Mexico for 71 consecutive years until 2000. Under PRI hegemony, Mexico City was governed by regentes (city managers) appointed directly by the president, with no elected local government until 1997.

events.title: Ciudad Universitaria (UNAM) Inaugurated
events.detail: On November 20, 1952, President Miguel Alemán inaugurated the new Ciudad Universitaria campus of UNAM in Pedregal de San Ángel, designed by Mario Pani and Enrique del Moral. The sprawling campus — declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2007 — featured murals by Diego Rivera, Juan O'Gorman, and David Alfaro Siqueiros. It became the symbolic and intellectual heart of Mexico's academic life and student political movements.

events.title: Tlatelolco Massacre — State Violence Against Students
events.detail: On October 2, 1968 — ten days before the Mexico City Olympic Games opening — government security forces and armed plainclothes agents (the Olimpia Battalion) surrounded a student rally at the Plaza de las Tres Culturas in Tlatelolco and opened fire. Official records stated 25 dead; independent investigations by the CNDH and journalists estimate 300–500 killed, with thousands arrested, tortured, or disappeared. The massacre was covered up for decades by the PRI government. In 1998, the Interamerican Commission on Human Rights formally condemned Mexico for the killings. The site remains a place of annual commemoration.

sources.name: National Security Archive / George Washington University
Elena Poniatowska — La Noche de Tlatelolco

events.confidenceReason: Official death count (25) is contradicted by decades of journalistic and governmental investigations suggesting 300–500 killed

events.title: 1968 Summer Olympics — First in Latin America
events.detail: Mexico City hosted the XIX Summer Olympic Games (October 12–27, 1968), the first Olympics held in Latin America and in a developing country. The Games were marked by the shadow of the Tlatelolco massacre ten days before opening. American athletes Tommie Smith and John Carlos raised fists in the Black Power salute during the 200m medal ceremony. The Games left lasting infrastructure including the Estadio Olímpico Universitario, the Palacio de los Deportes, and Azteca Stadium.

events.title: Great Earthquake — 10,000+ Killed, Civil Society Mobilizes
events.detail: At 7:19 AM on September 19, 1985, a magnitude 8.1 earthquake struck near the Pacific coast of Michoacán, devastating Mexico City. The city sits on the ancient lakebed of Lake Texcoco, which dramatically amplified seismic waves — a phenomenon known as the 'Mexico City effect.' At least 412 buildings collapsed and 3,124 were severely damaged. The official death toll was ~5,000 (certified bodies), but citizen groups estimate 10,000–40,000. President Miguel de la Madrid delayed response 39 hours and declined foreign aid initially. Citizens self-organized rescue brigades — a pivotal moment in Mexican civil society. The earthquake is credited with catalyzing democratic demand for elected city government.
events.confidenceReason: Government suppressed true death toll; estimates range 5,000–40,000

events.title: 1988 Electoral Fraud — 'Se Cayó el Sistema'
events.detail: In the July 1988 presidential election, Cuauhtémoc Cárdenas (running for the FDN coalition) was widely believed to have defeated PRI candidate Carlos Salinas. As vote counting showed Cárdenas leading, officials announced that the Federal Electoral Commission's computer system 'fell' (se cayó el sistema). When results resumed, Salinas was declared the winner with 50.7%. The fraud — later admitted by former president Zedillo — sparked massive protests in the Zócalo and fueled the PRD's formation, fundamentally delegitimizing the PRI in Mexico City.
events.confidenceReason: Official results declared Salinas winner; former President Zedillo later acknowledged systematic fraud

events.title: First Direct Election for Jefe de Gobierno — Cárdenas Wins
events.detail: Following decades of citizen pressure accelerated by the 1985 earthquake and 1988 fraud, constitutional reforms granted Mexico City residents the right to directly elect their Jefe de Gobierno in 1997. On July 6, 1997, Cuauhtémoc Cárdenas of the PRD won in a landslide — receiving 48% of votes vs 25% for PRI. This ended 68 years of presidential appointees governing the city. The election was seen as the beginning of Mexico's democratic transition, with CDMX as its leading edge.

events.title: AMLO Elected Jefe de Gobierno
events.detail: Andrés Manuel López Obrador (AMLO) of the PRD won the Mexico City mayoral election in July 2000, taking office in December. His government launched the first 'Programa de Apoyo para Adultos Mayores' (pension for the elderly), built the Segundo Piso (elevated highway), and implemented security reforms. His high approval ratings in CDMX made him the leading opposition candidate. He launched Metro Line 12 construction. AMLO governed until 2005 when he resigned to run for president.

sources.name: Gobierno de la CDMX — Informes de Gobierno 2000-2005
La Jornada — AMLO en la CDMX

events.title: AMLO Desafuero — Attempted Political Disqualification
events.detail: In April 2005, the federal congress voted to strip AMLO of his political immunity (desafuero) over a land dispute, which would have prevented him from running for president. The move, backed by President Fox and the PAN, triggered massive protests — up to 1.2 million people marched to the Zócalo on April 24, 2005, in one of Mexico City's largest-ever demonstrations. International pressure and public outcry forced the government to drop the charges. AMLO resigned as Jefe de Gobierno and launched his presidential campaign.

sources.name: El Universal / Reforma / La Jornada — Desafuero 2005

events.title: CDMX First in Latin America to Legalize Same-Sex Marriage
events.detail: On December 21, 2009, the CDMX Legislative Assembly (then dominated by PRD) approved civil same-sex marriage and adoption rights — making Mexico City the first jurisdiction in Latin America to do so. The law took effect in March 2010. The Catholic Church and the federal PAN government challenged the law before the Supreme Court, which upheld it in August 2010. The decision transformed CDMX into a leading destination for LGBTQ+ rights and tourism, and set a precedent later adopted nationwide.

events.title: CDMX Adopts Its Own Constitution — Becomes Autonomous Entity
events.detail: On January 31, 2017, the Constituyente (constitutional assembly) of Mexico City approved the first constitution of Ciudad de México, transforming it from the Distrito Federal into an autonomous political entity with full statehood-equivalent powers. The constitution incorporated extensive human rights provisions including the rights to water, housing, health, digital connectivity, and free abortion (a world first for a city constitution). It took effect February 5, 2017.

sources.name: Constitución Política de la Ciudad de México (2017)

events.title: 7.1 Earthquake Strikes on Anniversary of 1985 Disaster
events.detail: At 1:14 PM on September 19, 2017 — the exact 32nd anniversary of the 1985 earthquake — a magnitude 7.1 earthquake struck near Axochiapan, Morelos, devastating Mexico City. At least 369 people died nationwide; 187 in CDMX. More than 50 buildings collapsed in neighborhoods including Roma, Condesa, Del Valle, and Xochimilco. Ciudadanos organizados (civilian volunteers) spontaneously joined rescue efforts under the 'Mole' rescue coordination signal. Over 12,000 structures were damaged.

sources.name: Gobierno de la CDMX — Informe sismo 19S

events.title: Marcelo Ebrard Elected Jefe de Gobierno
events.detail: Marcelo Ebrard Casaubón (PRD) won the 2006 CDMX election and served as Jefe de Gobierno until 2012. His administration launched the Metrobús BRT system (2005, continued and expanded), Ecobici bike-sharing program, PREPA-CDMX adult high school, installed security cameras citywide, and continued progressive social policies including abortion decriminalization (2007, first in Mexico) and same-sex marriage (2009). Ebrard later served as AMLO's foreign minister (2018-2023).

sources.name: Gobierno de la CDMX — Informes de Gobierno 2006-2012

events.title: Metro Line 12 (Línea Dorada) Inaugurated
events.detail: Metro Line 12 (the Golden Line) was inaugurated by outgoing Jefe de Gobierno Marcelo Ebrard on October 30, 2012, connecting Tláhuac in the southeast to Mixcoac. The 24.7-km line with 20 stations was Mexico's largest infrastructure project in decades, costing MXN 26.3 billion. Almost immediately, structural defects emerged. Sections were closed in 2014 for repairs. On May 3, 2021, an overpass section between Olivos and Nopalera stations collapsed, killing 26 people and injuring 98.

events.title: Claudia Sheinbaum Elected — First Female Jefa de Gobierno
events.detail: On July 1, 2018, climate scientist and MORENA candidate Claudia Sheinbaum Pardo won the CDMX election with 47.1% of the vote, becoming the first woman elected as head of government of Mexico City. She took office October 5, 2018. Her administration continued AMLO-era social programs, expanded the Metro (Línea 3 extension), launched the Cablebús cable car transit system in the hills, and implemented security reforms that reduced homicide rates. She won international recognition for CDMX's climate action plan.

sources.name: Gobierno de la CDMX — Informes Sheinbaum 2018-2023

events.title: #UnDíaSinNosotras — Feminist Strike Against Femicide
events.detail: On March 9, 2020, women across Mexico participated in '#UnDíaSinNosotras' (A Day Without Us), a feminist strike against femicide and gender-based violence. In Mexico City, an estimated 80,000–120,000 women marched through the streets; many workplaces and public buildings were run by men for a day. The movement spray-painted murals and feminist messages on the Palacio Nacional and Supreme Court facades. Days later, the COVID-19 lockdown further constrained movement but not the underlying social demands.

sources.name: Animal Político / La Jornada — Paro Nacional Feminista 2020
Amnesty International — México: Violencia contra mujeres 2020

events.title: COVID-19 Pandemic — CDMX Becomes National Epicenter
events.detail: Mexico City declared a public health emergency in March 2020 as COVID-19 hit. CDMX became Mexico's hardest-hit city, accounting for a disproportionate share of national deaths. The Sheinbaum government implemented a traffic-light alert system and contact tracing. IMSS and ISSSTE hospital networks were overwhelmed. Excess mortality data from INEGI suggests CDMX experienced 70,000+ excess deaths above normal during 2020-2021. The pandemic exposed deep inequalities in healthcare access across the city's 16 alcaldías.

sources.name: Secretaría de Salud CDMX — Boletines diarios

events.title: Metro Line 12 Overpass Collapse — 26 Killed
events.detail: At 10:22 PM on May 3, 2021, a 220-meter section of the elevated guideway on Metro Line 12 between Olivos and Nopalera stations in Tláhuac collapsed, sending two train cars crashing 5 meters to the road below. Twenty-six people were killed and 98 injured in the deadliest Mexico City Metro accident since 1975. Norwegian firm DNV's forensic investigation found the collapse was caused by design flaws and deficient bolt installation during the original 2012 construction. As of 2025, the Fiscalía CDMX has filed charges against 10 former officials but no one has been imprisoned.

sources.name: Fiscalía General de Justicia CDMX — Informe L12

events.title: Cutzamala Water Crisis — Historic Low, 'Day Zero' Feared
events.detail: In early 2024, severe drought depleted the Cutzamala water system — which supplies 30% of CDMX's water — to its lowest recorded level: 30% capacity at the Valle de Bravo reservoir (May 2024). Mexico City set all-time temperature records (34.7°C, May 25, 2024) as the crisis deepened. Authorities warned of a potential 'Day Zero' in June 2024. Millions of CDMX residents already received water only 2-3 days per week via trucks. The 2024 rainy season partially replenished reservoirs, with levels recovering to ~52% by 2025. The crisis highlighted the city's fundamental water vulnerability: 70% extracted from aquifers, 40% lost to leaks, and ground subsidence of 50cm/yr in some zones.

sources.name: CNN / Al Jazeera / NBC News — CDMX water crisis

events.title: Claudia Sheinbaum Elected Mexico's First Female President
events.detail: On June 2, 2024, Claudia Sheinbaum — former Jefa de Gobierno of CDMX (2018-2023) — won the Mexican presidential election by a historic margin of 59.4% (35 million votes), becoming Mexico's first female president. She took office October 1, 2024, inheriting leadership of MORENA's continued transformation of Mexico. Her CDMX experience in security reform, climate policy, and transit expansion informed her presidential platform. She was succeeded as Jefa de Gobierno by Clara Brugada.

events.title: Clara Brugada Elected Jefa de Gobierno of CDMX
events.detail: On June 2, 2024, Clara Brugada Molina of MORENA won the CDMX gubernatorial election with ~52% of the vote, defeating PAN's Santiago Taboada. Brugada, a former alcaldesa of Iztapalapa (Mexico City's largest borough, ~1.8M people), took office October 5, 2024. Her administration proposed a 2025 budget of MXN 291.5 billion (+8.8%) with historic investments in Metro modernization (MXN 23B), security cameras, social programs, and water infrastructure. She announced plans to accelerate Metro expansion and Cablebús lines.

sources.name: Gobierno de la CDMX — Infobae / El Universal

events.title: Historic MXN 23B Metro Modernization Plan Approved
events.detail: In December 2024, the CDMX legislature approved the largest Metro investment in the system's history: MXN 23 billion for 2025 alone. The plan includes replacement of all escalators across the network, major maintenance on Lines 3 and A, installation of a dedicated security camera center (C2) covering all 226 km of track and 195+ stations, and studies for the modernization and extension of multiple lines. The investment comes four years after the Line 12 collapse and amid union accusations of chronic underinvestment.
